textDisplay:
This movie is beautiful. There are some major changes from the original poem but I like the different interpretations Lowery made. The themes of time, death, “honor,” Christianity & Paganism, nature & civilization, etc etc are present.

If you’re wanting fast-paced Avengers-style humor and action then you might want to skip it. If you want a slow, aesthetic wet dream then this is for you.
textOriginal:
This movie is beautiful. There are some major changes from the original poem but I like the different interpretations Lowery made. The themes of time, death, “honor,” Christianity & Paganism, nature & civilization, etc etc are present.

If you’re wanting fast-paced Avengers-style humor and action then you might want to skip it. If you want a slow, aesthetic wet dream then this is for you.

textDisplay: Saw this two nights ago. Absolutely incredible. I will say that it's not for everyone - I saw many people leaving the theater unhappy who I imagine expected something more conventional. But if you think it is for you, you have gotta see it. Beautiful, mesmerizing, and introspective. This is also a movie that benefits from the theater experience (I would compare it in this respect to something like Blade Runner 2049), so if you feel safe with all the virus stuff, try to catch it on the big screen. I am going to see it again tomorrow.
textOriginal: Saw this two nights ago. Absolutely incredible. I will say that it's not for everyone - I saw many people leaving the theater unhappy who I imagine expected something more conventional. textDisplay: This movie was so good but the theatre I went to was actually garbage. It was inside the mall and the mall was closed at the time of the movie and the guy on the intercom wasn’t paying attention for 10 minutes so we missed the opening scenes. The projector also cut out a whole 5 minutes of the movie and the manager rewinded only 10 seconds. Thirdly, the projector cut out the exact final 30 seconds of the movie and they didn’t even rewind it and as were were leaving they said sorry for the lamp issues. We weren’t even compensated and I’m writing a formal complaint as we speak. Safe to say I’m never going back there

textDisplay: Just watched this movie today. I won't give any spoilers, but the general set of this movie is a lot like what-if scenarios. It was a bit irritating at first, however, as the movie progresses you are able to tell what actually has happened a lot better. Definitely a thought-provoking movie, like Inception with Leonardo DiCaprio, or the Matrix. I took a few college courses where thought experiments were introduced and this definitely has quite a few of them in store.

textDisplay:
Film was great, just don't go in expecting it to be full of action, or be spoon-fed what you're supposed to think. It's a visually stunning movie with a very ambiguous message that leaves you thinking long after you leave the theater.
Also, no spoilers, but it adds, and alters certain things from the original poem to make it a more interesting film that leaves you on your toes even if you're familiar with the source material.
textOriginal:
Film was great, just don't go in expecting it to be full of action, or be spoon-fed what you're supposed to think. It's a visually stunning movie with a very ambiguous message that leaves you thinking long after you leave the theater.
Also, no spoilers, but it adds, and alters certain things from the original poem to make it a more interesting film that leaves you on your toes even if you're familiar with the source material.

textDisplay: I am quite happy (and not too terribly surprised) to see Dev Patel in the main role. I daresay he fits this sort of staggering narrative like a glove. Something about the performance--I think largely from his eyes--tells the audience of this trailer how quickly his determination fires up and drains away in the instant that he realizes the trap he walked into. Very accurate, I'd say, to the spirit of the character Gawain. And the way that fey and otherworldly folk are portrayed with a sense of distance and ghoulishness are quite thrilling.
textOriginal: I am quite happy (and not too terribly surprised) to see Dev Patel in the main role. I daresay he fits this sort of staggering narrative like a glove. Something about the performance--I think largely from his eyes--tells the audience of this trailer how quickly his determination fires up and drains away in the instant that he realizes the trap he walked into. Very accurate, I'd say, to the spirit of the character Gawain. And the way that fey and otherworldly folk are portrayed with a sense of distance and ghoulishness are quite thrilling.

textDisplay: This is not a troll comment...Wojak is 100% correct. This movie should have been released in the fall during Oscar-bait season. The trailer oversold this movie as far as pacing and a coherent plot goes.

textDisplay: @hugh7547 Agreed. This is probably the first time I've been as pissed off at how much the trailer misrepresented the movie. textDisplay:
Okay, so... I did a bit of digging myself into the actual story this is based off of.
So, here we have our main character, Gawain. Unlike his other companions in the order, he was very... Average. Not quite a leader like Lancelot, not evil like Mordred... Just kinda meh. He stood by his code, did what needed doing. When the titular Green Knight (no name noted) came into town, offering his axe to any who would strike him... IF, in one year's time, they are prepared to take a blow in return. Gawain, wanting to prove himself, accepted, managing to- as this trailer shows- cut his head clean off. The Green Knight then picks his head up, gives him directions to where he'll be- labelled as a Green Chapel, not a forested area- to receive the returning blow. A year passes, and he sets off to meet the Green Knight, confronting several trials. One of the main ones listed is that of Lady Bertilak and her spouse. When the spouse asks Gawain to keep his wife company, he obliges, wanting to be a good person... Only to then find out that Lady Bertilak REEEAAALLY wanted to seduce him. According to the tale, he managed to convince her that just a kiss on the cheek would be okay, receiving the show of affection and then meeting with the spouse to give him said kiss on the cheek in turn. Once that was done, Gawain headed back off, finally meeting with the Green Knight. Once he does, he kneels, awaiting the blow.
The Green Knight, rearing to take off his head, goes to strike... And instead just nicks the side of his neck, just a little. You see, Lady Bertilak and the spouse were actually the Green Knight in disguise, testing him to see if he was worthy of the Green Knight's axe. The only reason he got a nick instead of nothing at all was because Gawain was willing to let Lady Bertilak kiss his cheek instead of do nothing at all.

It's going to be hard for them to do this story justice, and from the looks of things, they're already trying to theatricalize it... Which is not a good sign. I wouldn't trust this movie as nothing more than eye candy, folks.

So, here we have our main character, Gawain. Unlike his other companions in the order, he was very... Average. Not quite a leader like Lancelot, not evil like Mordred... Just kinda meh. He stood by his code, did what needed doing. When the titular Green Knight (no name noted) came into town, offering his axe to any who would strike him... IF, in one year's time, they are prepared to take a blow in return. Gawain, wanting to prove himself, accepted, managing to- as this trailer shows- cut his head clean off. The Green Knight then picks his head up, gives him directions to where he'll be- labelled as a Green Chapel, not a forested area- to receive the returning blow. A year passes, and he sets off to meet the Green Knight, confronting several trials. One of the main ones listed is that of Lady Bertilak and her spouse. When the spouse asks Gawain to keep his wife company, he obliges, wanting to be a good person... Only to then find out that Lady Bertilak REEEAAALLY wanted to seduce him. According to the tale, he managed to convince her that just a kiss on the cheek would be okay, receiving the show of affection and then meeting with the spouse to give him said kiss on the cheek in turn. The Green Knight, rearing to take off his head, goes to strike... And instead just nicks the side of his neck, just a little. You see, Lady Bertilak and the spouse were actually the Green Knight in disguise, testing him to see if he was worthy of the Green Knight's axe. The only reason he got a nick instead of nothing at all was because Gawain was willing to let Lady Bertilak kiss his cheek instead of do nothing at all.

textDisplay: @LeonBelmont1000 Brainwashing? I mean, its nice to have a King Arthur lore movie where the setting looks right and colourful. I don't particularly care for the diverse casting no, especially when there were at least four knights of the round table who were people of colour anyway. textDisplay: @TalutellenduUle I just find it weird that they target a welsh/germanic character instead of having him play Palamedes or something that makes sense. Not saying medieval era was white only, but where else do you see this type of treatment for say, African mythos, or Chinese mythos casting white people instead of a predominant majority? It's only happening to cultures that were/are European to virtue signal.
textOriginal: @TalutellenduUle I just find it weird that they target a welsh/germanic character instead of having him play Palamedes or something that makes sense. Not saying medieval era was white only, but where else do you see this type of treatment for say, African mythos, or Chinese mythos casting white people instead of a predominant majority? It's only happening to cultures that were/are European to virtue signal.

textDisplay:
@LeonBelmont1000 Well, to be fair, The Arthurian tales are not Welsh alone (and in the Welsh canon, Arthur himself was supposedly from Yorkshire, according to the originator of the stories, Geoffrey of Monmouth) , but English too; especially the more famous canon that we think of as Arthurian, the whole 'Sword in the stone' angle, the knights of the round table, etc; all of that is the work of Thomas Malory in the 13th century. This particular story, Gawain and the Green Knight, is believed to have come from somewhere along the English and Welsh border. 

Not to mention as you said the Germanic and French influences, and the stories from Brittany. And the Cornish lay claim to Arthur's origins as well. So in other words, these stories mean a lot to Western Europe in general.

 Put simply, he and his knights are product of Western Europe as a whole, which was 99.9999 percent white. Despite what some might claim, you would be very unusual if you met a single black person in your life during the very broad post-Roman Medieval to renaissance period. So yes, i myself roll my eyes slightly at the diverse casting, because you know the excuse is ''But none of this is real, its based on legend!'' but those legends are based around a real people's tales and myths. So I agree with you, in that I don't particularly like the diverse casting approach which does only seem to be a thing with European stories. It's a rather hypocritical stance to have. 

For me, credit where it is due; I like the look of the film, I like the look of the green knight, I like the very strong fantasy elements after years of 'realistic' Arthurian movies where everything is brown and depressing. But the casting is a shame, and I get past it by just considering it a kind of stage play, where your ethnicity, traditionally, did not matter. It's disappointing for sure, but I know it could have been worse, and often is. But maybe one day we'll get the faultless portrayal we deserve.
textOriginal:

This particular story, Gawain and the Green Knight, is believed to have come from somewhere along the English and Welsh border. 

Not to mention as you said the Germanic and French influences, and the stories from Brittany. And the Cornish lay claim to Arthur's origins as well. So in other words, these stories mean a lot to Western Europe in general.

 Put simply, he and his knights are product of Western Europe as a whole, which was 99.9999 percent white. Despite what some might claim, you would be very unusual if you met a single black person in your life during the very broad post-Roman Medieval to renaissance period. So yes, i myself roll my eyes slightly at the diverse casting, because you know the excuse is ''But none of this is real, its based on legend!'' but those legends are based around a real people's tales and myths. So I agree with you, in that I don't particularly like the diverse casting approach which does only seem to be a thing with European stories. It's a rather hypocritical stance to have. 

For me, credit where it is due; I like the look of the film, I like the look of the green knight, I like the very strong fantasy elements after years of 'realistic' Arthurian movies where everything is brown and depressing. But the casting is a shame, and I get past it by just considering it a kind of stage play, where your ethnicity, traditionally, did not matter. textDisplay:
Why did they cast an Indian to play a British knight?
Gawain was the son of King Lot of Orkney and Morgause who was maternal halfsister to King Arthur. 
His brothers were Agravain, Gaheris, Gareth, and Mordred.  
In some stories, Mordred is his maternal half brother and son of King Arthur. 

There was a 1984 Gawain and Green Knight film starring Miles O'Keefe. 
It's Sword of the Valiant. 
It's a mediocre movie.
textOriginal:

There was a 1984 Gawain and Green Knight film starring Miles O'Keefe.

For unto us a child is born, unto us a son is given: and the government shall be upon his shoulder: and his name shall be called Wonderful, Counsellor, The mighty God, *The everlasting Father,* The Prince of Peace.

Matthew 1:23
Behold, a virgin shall be with child, and shall bring forth a son, and they shall call his name Emmanuel which being interpreted is, *God with us.*

John 1:1 & 14
1 In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.
14 And the Word was made flesh (Lord Jesus), and dwelt among us, (and we beheld his glory, the glory as of the only begotten of the Father,) full of grace and truth.

John 8:24 (Jesus speaking)
I said therefore unto you, that ye shall die in your sins: for if ye believe not that I am he, ye shall die in your sins.

John 14:9 (Jesus speaking)
Jesus saith unto him, Have I been so long time with you, and yet hast thou not known me, Philip? he that hath seen me hath seen the Father; and how sayest thou then, Show us the Father?

Hebrews 1:1-3, & 8 (God calls His Son "O God" because Jesus IS God in the flesh)
1 God, who at sundry times and in divers manners spake in time past unto the fathers by the prophets,
2 Hath in these last days spoken unto us by his Son, whom he hath appointed heir of all things, by whom also he made the worlds;
3 Who being the brightness of his glory, and the express image of his person, and upholding all things by the word of his power, when he had by himself purged our sins, sat down on the right hand of the Majesty on high
8 But unto the Son he saith, Thy throne, O God, is for ever and ever: a sceptre of righteousness is the sceptre of thy kingdom.

1 John 5:7
For there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost: and these three are one.

Titus 2:13
Looking for that blessed hope, and the glorious appearing of the great God and our Saviour Jesus Christ;

Revelation 1:7
Behold, he cometh with clouds; *and every eye shall see him* and they also which pierced him and all kindreds of the earth shall wail because of him Even so Amen

Isaiah 44:6 (God speaking)
Thus saith the LORD the King of Israel, and his redeemer the LORD of hosts; I am the first, and I am the last; and beside me there is no God.

Revelation 1:8 (Jesus speaking)
I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the ending, saith the Lord, which is, and which was, and which is to come, *the Almighty.*

Revelation 22:13 (Jesus speaking)
I am Alpha and Omega, the beginning and the end, the first and the last.

---

There Are None Righteous / How To Be Saved

Romans 3:10 & 23
10 As it is written, There is none righteous, no, not one:
23 For all have sinned, and come short of the glory of God;

Luke 5:31-32 (Jesus speaking)
31 And Jesus answering said unto them, They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick.
32 I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.

1 Peter 3:18 (The word “quicken” means “to make alive”)
For Christ also hath once suffered for sins, the just for the unjust, that he might bring us to God, being put to death in the flesh, but quickened by the Spirit:

Romans 10:9
That if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved.

Acts 4:12
Neither is there salvation in any other: for there is none other name under heaven given among men, whereby we must be saved.

Ephesians 2:8-9
8 For by grace are ye saved through faith; and that not of yourselves: it is the gift of God:
9 Not of works, lest any man should boast.

textDisplay:
Sir Gawaine and the Gween Knight
 By Sir Elmer De Fudd

Here is a stowy that is told in whyme,
About what had happened in a wong ago time,
How in Camewot Town, where Arthur did weign,
Wived a wusty young knight y-clept Sir Gawaine.

Now Camewot folk were hale and hearty,
Always a lookout for weasons to party,
A mawwiage, a death, sometimes a divorce,
Or even the gelding of a knight's bwand new horse.

So 'twas somebody's birthday, or maybe their pup's,
And the knights hard at work, were deep in their cups,
When fwom the fwont portal a woar did awise,
And in wode a knight of incwedible size.

His armour, his barding, his twappings were gween,
And his waiment did gwow with a silky gween sheen.
He wifted his gauntwet and waised up his vizor,
And in the deep gwoom gweamed two huge incisors.

Fwom the cavernous depths of that emewald hood,
Gwowled a voice that made everyone cwap where they stood,
"I am the Gween knight of the highest degwees,
Here to make bwave men go weak in their knees!"

"I come from a wineage both noble and wong,
A famiwy that's famed in stowy and song.
You've heard of my bwother, the fierce Sir Bwuce,
He is the knight who wears Owange and Peuce.

And many's the knight, both wiving and dead,
Who has met Bwadamante, the woman in wed.
Well, she is my sister, wenowned in gweat halls,
And few are the knights who deny she has balls.

I am here to discover, by cunning or might,
The measure of he who would call himself knight.
The bwavest of men, most handsome and able,
Is he one here feasting at Arthur's wound table?

For here is my pwan, you must consider it so.
The bwave knight and I will twade bwow for bwow,.
What he gives to me, I in my turn,
Will give back to him, and thus fame will earn.

Now Gawaine, bwave and bold, of this nothing knew.
All that he'd wanted was to wace to the woo,
He'd been dwinking since noon, and his bwadder was tight,
His need being urgent, ne'er looked left nor wight.

Fwom his seat at the table he made a mad dash,
But into a huge gween-mailed chest did he cwash,
He stwangled a yelp and dwew out his bwade,
And a wild swinging swash was the move that he made.

Now such was the force of that despewate wop,
That the Gween Knight's head fwom his shoulders did dwop.
With a thundewing cwash, that caused all to start,
And fwom habit Queen Guinevere's knees flew apart.

But the magic was stwong, and with one mighty bound,
The gwisly object weapt up fwom the gwound,
And perched back a-top, a picture of dwead,
And gwared at Gawaine, then snarled "Give me head."

This startled Gawaine, and fwom the state of the fwoor,
His concern was not with the woo any more.
His only thought was, oh, I do feel so queer.
But what to do next, well I've got no idea.

For when I was a student at Camewot U,
All that they taught was to hack and hew.
And none of the teachers, whether wicked or mowal,
So much as hinted at doing twicks owal.

But Queen Guinivere often made it quite pwain,
That she had a soft spot for poor Sir Gawain,
And her Wauncewot fwing had set the tongues waggin',
She even fancied her husband, King Arthur Pendwagon.

So Gwenny weaned over to give him a tip,
A wink and a nudge and a pout of the wip.
Gawain, she did whisper, here's my advice.
Wemember the twick that I did with the ice.

Gawaine yelled hooway, thwough a half stifle sob,
And shoved all the ice he could find in his gob.
And when it was gone, no more left in the bucket,
He faced his opponent and yelled Mffll mmfgmanm mfmm.

The emewald cwad knight did quietly advance,
And equally quietly Gawaine messed his pants,
Till they stood, face to chest, still nothing was said.
Then the Gween Knight's bwade washed at Gawaine's head.

The weapon exploded to bits in a twice,
The audience was spwattered with sawiva and ice,
But Gawaine just stood there, this knight of gweat style,
And sneered at his enemy with a pitying smile.

You fool, he decwared, have you never been told,
That the fight goes not to the swift nor the bold,
Fowwow a woman's advice, for that never fails,
And that is the coowest head always pwevails.

textDisplay: Okay, I know I am going to get a lot of crap for this but this is quintessential European folklore and culture right here. Why the hell is Dev Patel the lead? Can we please stop with all the virtue signaling and be somewhat accurate here? King Arthur’s nephew would not be a middle eastern brown dude. Sorry, not sorry. I understand it’s fantasy and yes, Dev Patel is a great actor but this is a slap in the face. And this kind of casting only ever seems to be fine with people when it’s European folklore and characters having their identities erased. This is such a deeply rooted and special piece of cultural heritage, and thus casting ruins it. All I am going to see is a giant virtue signal running around the screen and ruining the experience.

textDisplay:
To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father Esclabor and his two brothers Safir and Segwarides, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. 
And male bisexuality - which makes me wonder why the right-wingers are so interested in this. 🤫🤭

@maylabrown4584 To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father Esclabor and his two brothers Safir and Segwarides, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. 
And male bisexuality - which makes me wonder why the right-wingers are so interested in this story.

And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. 
And male bisexuality - which makes me wonder why the right-wingers are so interested in this story.

textDisplay: It's not a biopic, documentary, or presenting itself as "accurate to history/culture," so it's meaningless to agonize over race. The Witch and The Lighthouse were films that presented themselves as being accurate to the location and time period, so the race of the characters was important. This film is clearly taking many artistic liberties with an already fantastical story; you'll notice the clothing and settings are not really "accurate" either.

textDisplay: @terriblereading7586 The Gawain legend, along with other Arthurian legends, predates the 14th century and in fact predates the Roman invasion and foundation of the British empire. The mythos and characters are firmly Celtic Brythonic. However, they are still _myths_ and this is a film taking many artistic liberties, so the portrayal doesn't matter in this instance, but please don't try to rewrite a culture. Not much was preserved as it is.
textOriginal: @terriblereading7586 The Gawain legend, along with other Arthurian legends, predates the 14th century and in fact predates the Roman invasion and foundation of the British empire. The mythos and characters are firmly Celtic Brythonic. However, they are still _myths_ and this is a film taking many artistic liberties, so the portrayal doesn't matter in this instance, but please don't try to rewrite a culture. Not much was preserved as it is.

textDisplay:
@HappyArnold-g5r I am a white person with Welsh grandparents who has a minor in English cultural studies, so I'm willing to bet I have a fair amount more investment in this than you, and I could not care less that an ethnically Indian man is playing Gawain in this film. You know why? _Because it's an artistic adaptation of a fantasy story._ It's not marketing itself as being "culturally and historically accurate." I wasn't mad when they made an adaptation of Romeo and Juliet set in a weird retro future with a black guy playing Mercutio, either, because it would be _actually insane_ to be upset about that.

You know what irked me? When they marketed Anne Boleyn as "historically accurate" and then cast a black woman to play the character. _That_ is an instance where being annoyed or upset at representation is reasonable. NOT an arthouse fantasy film based off a fictional mythology with f*cking tree monsters in it.

You know why? _Because it's an artistic adaptation of a fantasy story._ It's not marketing itself as being "culturally and historically accurate." I wasn't mad when they made an adaptation of Romeo and Juliet set in a weird retro future with a black guy playing Mercutio, either, because it would be _actually insane_ to be upset about that.

You know what irked me? textDisplay:
One more time for the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father Esclabor and his two brothers Safir and Segwarides, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. 
And male bisexuality - which makes me wonder why the right-wingers are so interested in this story.

And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father Esclabor and his two brothers Safir and Segwarides, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. 
And male bisexuality - which makes me wonder why the right-wingers are so interested in this story.

""""difference is : gawain is fictional and his skin color is neither quintessential for the fantasy story he is in, nor is it mentioned. That being said: no, I didn't like the casting choice for Boleyn.""""""

textDisplay:
@zedas1 - Tarsem had actually told the entire crew and even Catinca (the little girl Alexandria) that Roy (the character Lee Pace played) was a real person who was a paraplegic who was acting as himself in the movie :o So Lee Pace had to be pushed to the set in a wheelchair every day and carried by men just to move around. This was so the child could act better, and wouldn't jump on his legs or anything because she was scared of hurting him. So when you see him being angry at her and her getting afraid, her fear is real, or laughing or getting confused, it's often real reactions happening as the scene is being filmed. When she reads the 'e' in morphine as '3' that actually happened. They built the script and story around how Alexandria and "Roy's" conversations developed. Re-watching the movie knowing all this is a pretty incredible experience. And when they're in that blue city, rather than CGIing a blue city they actually found a city where the locals are only allowed to use blue paint. They gave away free blue paint to anyone in the city who wanted it, and the people who lived in the city literally painted it blue!!!!

All the fantasy scenes are filmed after the hospital scenes, after the story was already told. And by that time they had made Lee tell Cantica he could actually walk (he had to stand up and show her after they filmed the last hospital scene). At the time she thought it was because she loved him and somehow her love had healed him. I'm not entirely sure about the ethics of tricking a child in that way, and apparently some of the crew were furious, but in later interviews Cantinca has said she really appreciated and values the experience so yeah, no long-term damage done hopefully :)
textOriginal:
@zedas1 - Tarsem had actually told the entire crew and even Catinca (the little girl Alexandria) that Roy (the character Lee Pace played) was a real person who was a paraplegic who was acting as himself in the movie :o So Lee Pace had to be pushed to the set in a wheelchair every day and carried by men just to move around. This was so the child could act better, and wouldn't jump on his legs or anything because she was scared of hurting him. So when you see him being angry at her and her getting afraid, her fear is real, or laughing or getting confused, it's often real reactions happening as the scene is being filmed. When she reads the 'e' in morphine as '3' that actually happened. They built the script and story around how Alexandria and "Roy's" conversations developed. Re-watching the movie knowing all this is a pretty incredible experience. And when they're in that blue city, rather than CGIing a blue city they actually found a city where the locals are only allowed to use blue paint. They gave away free blue paint to anyone in the city who wanted it, and the people who lived in the city literally painted it blue!!!!

All the fantasy scenes are filmed after the hospital scenes, after the story was already told. And by that time they had made Lee tell Cantica he could actually walk (he had to stand up and show her after they filmed the last hospital scene). At the time she thought it was because she loved him and somehow her love had healed him. I'm not entirely sure about the ethics of tricking a child in that way, and apparently some of the crew were furious, but in later interviews Cantinca has said she really appreciated and values the experience so yeah, no long-term damage done hopefully :)

textDisplay: I love the original tale. And while I think a movie SHOULD reflect a director's unique creative vision (for example, portraying the Green Knight as an anthropomorphic tree is a creative choice, and not how the Green Knight is typically portrayed, but that's fine: the director could be arguing that the Green Knight represents nature or something), this looks like another ho-hum fantasy trading in Hollywood's version of medievalism. From the trailer alone, we can note these departures from the beloved folk tale: Arthur's Court is bleak, bored, and joyless, not colorful and festive. Arthur himself a frail old man, not the powerful knightly king (no doubt this will be the movie's explanation for why Gawain takes up the Green Knight's game in Arthur's stead). Unproven knights adorned with haloes (only saints get haloes in medieval art, people; and you only become a saint AFTER enduring many trials; at the start of the tale, Gawain is an untested knight). French cathedrals in an English folktale (yes, more cinematic, but Hollywood really enjoys tossing together a hodge-podge of stuff, so long as it "looks medieval"). In the original tale, "honor" was not the central theme. This is the chivalry code of medieval Christendom, not the Japanese Bushido "honor" code (not that Hollywood knows the difference). The five knightly virtues were: friendship, generosity, chastity, courtesy, and piety, and Gawain is tested in all five by the end of his quest. Honor barely features in the story.

From the trailer alone, we can note these departures from the beloved folk tale: Arthur's Court is bleak, bored, and joyless, not colorful and festive. Arthur himself a frail old man, not the powerful knightly king (no doubt this will be the movie's explanation for why Gawain takes up the Green Knight's game in Arthur's stead). Unproven knights adorned with haloes (only saints get haloes in medieval art, people; and you only become a saint AFTER enduring many trials; at the start of the tale, Gawain is an untested knight). French cathedrals in an English folktale (yes, more cinematic, but Hollywood really enjoys tossing together a hodge-podge of stuff, so long as it "looks medieval"). In the original tale, "honor" was not the central theme. This is the chivalry code of medieval Christendom, not the Japanese Bushido "honor" code (not that Hollywood knows the difference). The five knightly virtues were: friendship, generosity, chastity, courtesy, and piety, and Gawain is tested in all five by the end of his quest. Honor barely features in the story.

textDisplay:
To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality.

textDisplay: @AlecArtComics that would be great if they do that!! I haven't been paying much attention as I don't want to have my hopes dashed but I've been going through Dork Lords LOTR playlist and one of his videos I watched a few days ago was about how the series was adapting Aragorn's backstory. However that playlist plays from oldest to newest now that I think about it so you're right that video may have been quite old!!! I'd love it if they adapt Silmarillion even just the second age as it's truly an amazing book (one of the best) but at the same time I feel a lot of trepidation because I'm very pedantic about adaptations. Like what if they're too scared to show us how crazy elves can really be so just miss out all the kinslaying and massacres and all the insanity the elves cause over their pretty shiny things and their inability to let go of rage and pain and grudges? To me that's part of what makes elves way more awesome and to be honest I thought the way they portrayed Thranduil in the Hobbit trilogy as kind of crazy and unreasonable was much more how a lot of the Silmarillion elves are, haha, he was adapted brilliantly to represent the more unstable side of elfdom. Sad they made his gems belong to his wife though when they could have adapted the Nauglamir into the story instead! But yeah I'm worried they'd cut all that stuff and make all the elves noble and awesome like Glorfindel. Glorfindel is wonderful, but that capacity for darkness brings so much more character to elves. Also I'd have loved to have seen Morthgoth's mutilation of the elves before the first age, and Unguliant sucking the light from the world.. but yeah whatever they do if it's good I'll be happy. If it's terrible I'll just pretend I never knew they were making it so it doesn't have to exist in my world, haha. Cheers for the info!

textDisplay:
It's derived from Welsh mythology, therefore, they should be portrayed by Welsh people representative of the era in which the tales were written.

And don't be so coy. You fucking well know that Western media has been inserting blacks and other races into stories and mythologies of people who never had a single black person around during the time of their creation. It is an attempt to assert that it was *always* that northern and western Europe had other races. Which is a lie.

By your pathetic logic, you'd have no problem with Sudanese mythological beings being portrayed by an Irish lad. Methinks accusations of 'whitewashing' would abound.

It's derived from Welsh mythology, therefore, they should be portrayed by Welsh people representative of the era in which the tales were written.

And don't be so coy. You fucking well know that Western media has been inserting blacks and other races into stories and mythologies of people who never had a single black person around during the time of their creation. It is an attempt to assert that it was *always* that northern and western Europe had other races. Which is a lie.

By your pathetic logic, you'd have no problem with Sudanese mythological beings being portrayed by an Irish lad. Methinks accusations of 'whitewashing' would abound.

textDisplay:
@Novarcharesk To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality.

textDisplay:
@terriblereading7586 I suppose that’s a fair question since I had to open my big nerdy mouth and say something. For organization’s sake, let’s take the movie trailer scene by scene. (Spoilers for the poem and possibly? the movie ahead). The trailer opens with some creepy music and mystical scenes that I can only assume are meant to be Morgan le Fay doing some pagan magic to turn Lord Bertilak into the Green Knight. Over this, we hear King Arthur asking his knights to entertain him and Guinevere at their Christmas feast. Right on cue, the Green Knight (looking like Groot from hell) waltzes in to propose a beheading game. So far, it’s pretty accurate except for a few minor details. For one, in the poem the Green Knight comes riding in on a green horse, which I think is a shame they removed in the film because if you have an excuse to throw a green horse in a movie, why wouldn’t you want a green horse? But still, it’s not a big difference. The more curious difference is that in the movie we have a King Arthur who is quite old and probably near the end of his reign, which is an interesting choice since the poem actually takes place near the beginning of his reign as king and makes a big deal about how Arthur is so young and full of boyish energy. In fact, the whole mood of the scene is meant to be jovial and energetic (it is a Christmas party afterall) rather than dark and creepy. Of Arthur, we are told that “his youth made him so merry with the moods of a boy.” Perhaps this choice on the part of the filmmakers is to justify why Arthur didn’t take up the Knight’s challenge himself? Again, not a major difference, but worth noting. Moving on…

Gawain accepts the challenge and as he goes to cut off his head, we get another series of flashes that I think are meant to be a battle between Christian and pagan symbols. This is also appropriate to the poem. But then Gawain leaves for his journey after a pep-talk from Arthur and that’s where things start to get really weird. In the poem, Gawain puts off actually going on his quest until the very last minute as he is, understandably, not looking forward to having his head cut off. He then sets out to find the Green Chapel. What is fascinating, however, is that for a poem that took over 100 lines of poetry to describe his getting dressed for the journey, Gawain’s actual adventures while traveling are summed up in a montage of less than 50 lines of poetry, much of which is him wandering around asking random villages if they’ve heard of the Green Chapel, being pretty lonely, and listing some beasties he fought along the way. The real bulk of the poem has very little to do with this leg of his journey, and mostly takes place after he arrives at the castle of Lord and Lady Bertilak who tell him they know where he can find the Green Knight. The Bertilaks offer to let him chill with them until it’s time to go find the Green Knight and for the majority of the poem, Gawain is laying on a bed in their home having flirtatious conversations with Lady Bertilak while Lord Bertilak and his band of manly men are out doing some very gorey manly hunting. The only condition Lord Bertilak makes on his stay is that at the end of the day, Gawain gives back to him whatever he received that day. Each day, Lady Bertilak convinces Gawain to let her give him a kiss, and each day at the end of the day, Gawain gives a kiss to Lord Bertilak to fulfill his promise. The day before he leaves to actually face the Green Knight, Lady Bertilak convinces Gawain to take a green belt that supposedly has magic that will protect him. Against his better judgement, Gawain takes the belt and does NOT give it to Lord Bertilak at the end of the day, which breaks his promise to him (dun dun dun). 

When he actually goes out and meets the Green Knight the next day, the Green Knight swings his axe at Gawain’s neck and Gawain flinches. The Knight lays into Gawain about how he should be taking death like a man. Gawain tells him to swing again and assures him that this time he won’t move. The Knight swings and stops short of cutting off his head, just nicking him instead. He then merrily reveals that he is actually Lord Bertilak and that Morgan le Fay made him look like this in order to test Arthur’s court and also possibly to scare Guinevere to death. At this point, Bertilak gives Gawain a little scolding for taking the green belt, but on the whole thinks the entire thing was a great game, says he can come hang out with them anytime, and gives Gawain a B+ for knightliness. Gawain, on the other hand, is mortified. He rants about how all women suck and then goes home feeling like a failure, a point that is driven home by the fact that all the knights back in Camelot start wearing green belts to honor him, even though he tries to tell them that the green belt is where he failed. 

The poem is fascinating for its color symbolism and for how Gawain is actually kind of an awful knight, does not fulfill the stereotypical masculine gender roles well, and yet no one but him seems to have a problem with it. Morgan le Fay’s odd position as a shady background antagonist is also intriguing and her true motives for instigating the whole quest are the subject of much debate among scholars. 

In the movie trailer, we get none of this. Instead, it seems to capitalize on those 50 brief lines of poetry where Gawain is off adventuring before he runs into the Bertilaks. We see all kinds of random scenes such as Gawain bound and gagged while some sassy boy says he will finish his quest on his behalf, hear him talking to a creepy fox who, strangely enough, talks back, and see him interacting with bald, naked giants. Then there are some artsy scenes that seem to be trying to blur reality with Gawain’s fears, such as scenes where the camera is spinning in circles or where Gawain’s head is on fire. What bothered me most however, is that in a separate trailer for the film, the narrator actually claims that Morgan le Fay is Gawain’s mother. This is not true: Morgause was Gawain’s mother. This is a very big deal since Morgan le Fay is, as previously stated, the background antagonist for the adventure with motives that are convoluted and the subject of much debate. If they are going to play it that she’s his mom rather than drawing from one of the many possible valid interpretations of le Fay’s character, it will likely either turn her into some kind of demented super-villain torturing her son, or create some whole other plot that just simply does not exist. Either way, it strips Morgan of any of the possible, really interesting reasons she could have had for orchestrating the whole thing.

@sappho114 Yes, I am happy to have provided you with this information. The human imagination is a peculiar beast, in this case, I want to "believe" I am watching Arthurian romance, this is different from literal "belief" that Arthurian romance is a factual account of anything. It is itself part of history, in this case we would wish to be transported to the world of 14th-century courtly romance. 
As I have already said, I liked Patel in Chappie. He is a good actor and he did nothing wrong (except maybe be a willing participant in this nihilist drive to purge European culture), it wasn't his choice to leave Gujarat and become a "brown Englishman" as you describe it.

Hey there.

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
Two things JVR, as you've got your knickers in a twist....

1. I'm not saying that there wasn't a small admixture from the corners of the Roman Empire and Mediterranean trade itself introduced into Britain over time. There's evidence of that through archaeological finds. Although that evidence is NOT that vast numbers of settlers from the middle east arrived here, but rather that a few soldiers or their retinue did, and that goods were traded suggesting some possible immigration, on a small scale. It's been rebranded as something else for 21st century woke purposes.

2. The medieval writings do mention Saracens. But the story is set in Dark Ages Britain. The Saracens were hundreds of years hence.

3. It jars, because Sir Gawain is not described as being Indian. And the chances are very high that he didn't look classically Indian, Chinese or Chilean. It jars like the strawberry-blond Jesus used to on the mural in the church I went to as a boy. It jars like Alec Guinness as King Faisal in Lawrence of Arabia, or Liz Taylor as Cleopatra. 

Yes, Gawain is known through a mythical poem, but he is rooted in a historical time, when the ancient Britons invited then tussles with the new arrivals, the Germanic peoples who became known as the English. I know that isn't a fashionable theory anymore but it's broadly  backed up genetically, geographically and linguistically. 

It doesn't mean I don't approve of Dev Patel - I do, he's a great actor. 

It just means that if your pitching something in the past, then perhaps historical accuracy jars less.

textDisplay:
To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
@HawcProductions 
To quote the original poem:
"Sumwhyle wyth wormez he werrez, and with wolues als,
Sumwhyle wyth wodwos, þat woned in þe knarrez,
Boþe wyth bullez and berez, and borez oþerquyle,
And ETAYNEZ, þat hym anelede of þe heȝe felle;"

The poem refers to foes of Camelot using a Germanic term for giant: the Middle English etayn, from the Old English eoten and cognate with the Old Norse jötunn.
In modern translations they are not always referred to as Giants but as Ogres or such but the original text makes it pretty clear: Gawain meets dragons, wolves, wild men, bulls, bears, boars and Etaynez/Giants.

The poem refers to foes of Camelot using a Germanic term for giant: the Middle English etayn, from the Old English eoten and cognate with the Old Norse jötunn.

textDisplay: @terriblereading7586 probably what they had in mind, but "giant" and "ogre" are just two possible latinate translations of eoten, and "ogre" is arguably even better, as "eoten" does not necessarily indicate gigantic stature, it's apparently a term for a "voracious monster" of some description, for which "ogre" is probably a better approximation than "naked hairless giant".
textOriginal: @terriblereading7586 probably what they had in mind, but "giant" and "ogre" are just two possible latinate translations of eoten, and "ogre" is arguably even better, as "eoten" does not necessarily indicate gigantic stature, it's apparently a term for a "voracious monster" of some description, for which "ogre" is probably a better approximation than "naked hairless giant".

It's based on a 14th century poem.

Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. textDisplay:
To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century."
Well, there is also a half-black Arthurian Knight in the most important Medieval Germanic Arthurian Myth and it's 200 years older than the source material for this movie. The knights name is Feirefiz and he is the half-brother of Sir Parcival.
Also Patel is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
@terriblereading7586 You are really stretching here. You also seem to be completely ignorant of the nature of Islam in Spain. The so-called Spanish Arabs were in fact the descendants of white North African Berbers diluted with many generations of local Spanish women, often taken as slaves. They did not form a distinct racial type.

The term "Moor" is a ridiculous misnomer. In common parlance, it means Spanish Muslims, but in fact, the historical term really refers to the Almoravids, invaders from Senegal in sub-Saharan Africa. The Almoravids conquered much of Spain in the late 11th century, long after the Arthurian legends were conceived. Naturally, many of these newcomers, essentially the Taliban of their day, were black. This gives rise to the misconception that the Spanish Arabs were dark.

But they would have had no place among the Christian nobility of England.

Personally, I think that King Arthur was Jewish. So was Julius Caesar and Alexander the Great.

Hey brothers and sisters, I love y'all and it's just been in my heart today to share that Jesus love y'all so much and unconditionally. I don't know how much time we left on this earth before Jesus comes, but I want all of us to be ready when He does. Let's leave these things of this world behind for in scripture it says in Matthew 24:35 that heaven and earth will disappear, but my words will never disappear. " Also in 2 corinthians 4:18" So we fix our eyes not on what is seen, but on what is unseen, since what is seen is temporary, but what is unseen is eternal." Time is so short. And I always say this" I want all of us to be saved. " Jesus is coming back soon." with tears in my eyes I love you guys. Let's take Jesus and His words seriously in these time were in. I know the road isn't easy because there are so much distractions and temptations in this life. But through prayer and fasting we can overcome them. I don't want to force anything on anybody or even force them to do something they don't. But if you were to see your mother about to get hit by a big truck, you would do everything to try and save her. So I do the same thing for us about the wrath of God that is to come. I don't want anyone I love, know/don't know or even those who hate or dislike me to face such things. Because I know every soul is precious to God and are valuable to Him. Because before we were born He know us, He knitted us in our mother's womb, He called us to be His children . His love for us is so great and so strong, it's beyond human comprehension, that even the angels in heaven don't understand it. So I want to tell you it's time to get ready, time to prepare ourselves for the Second Coming of Christ, for the trails and tribulations and for the rapture. As you can see, so many people have turned away from God. The world has become so immoral, wicked, disgusting and violent. Killing people without any care or heart. And that shows that the love of God is growing cold in the hearts of people. Please be ready for when the Son of Man comes. Time is running out. Let's not leave this world with regrets. Because I tell you this, my biggest regret is not knowing Jesus Christ earlier. I love you Guys and Jesus loves you too, that's why He didn't think twice about dying on the Cross for us, knowing that we may not even love Him back. Please take what was said today in consideration because ready or not Jesus Christ is coming back believe it or not Jesus is coming back. 

How to be saved? To be saved, a man must confess that Jesus is Lord while acknowledging in his heart that Christ must have full rule over his life and that he died on the cross for our sins and was resurrected on the third day🙏🏾. Also one must repent and turn away from thier sin. NOW, is the time to turn to JESUS, do it before its too LATE and the DOOR of Grace is Shut.

Prayer of Salvation in Jesus Christ🙏

Dear Lord Jesus, I know that I am a sinner, and I ask for Your forgiveness. I believe You died for my sins and rose from the dead. I turn from my sins and invite You to come into my heart and life. I want to trust and follow You as my Lord and Savior.

Spreading the Gospel on every content🙏❤️.

And I always say this" I want all of us to be saved. " Jesus is coming back soon." with tears in my eyes I love you guys. Let's take Jesus and His words seriously in these time were in. I know the road isn't easy because there are so much distractions and temptations in this life. But through prayer and fasting we can overcome them. I don't want to force anything on anybody or even force them to do something they don't. But if you were to see your mother about to get hit by a big truck, you would do everything to try and save her. So I do the same thing for us about the wrath of God that is to come. I don't want anyone I love, know/don't know or even those who hate or dislike me to face such things. Because I know every soul is precious to God and are valuable to Him. Because before we were born He know us, He knitted us in our mother's womb, He called us to be His children . His love for us is so great and so strong, it's beyond human comprehension, that even the angels in heaven don't understand it. So I want to tell you it's time to get ready, time to prepare ourselves for the Second Coming of Christ, for the trails and tribulations and for the rapture. As you can see, so many people have turned away from God. The world has become so immoral, wicked, disgusting and violent. Killing people without any care or heart. And that shows that the love of God is growing cold in the hearts of people. Please be ready for when the Son of Man comes. Time is running out. Let's not leave this world with regrets. Because I tell you this, my biggest regret is not knowing Jesus Christ earlier. I love you Guys and Jesus loves you too, that's why He didn't think twice about dying on the Cross for us, knowing that we may not even love Him back. Please take what was said today in consideration because ready or not Jesus Christ is coming back believe it or not Jesus is coming back.

To be saved, a man must confess that Jesus is Lord while acknowledging in his heart that Christ must have full rule over his life and that he died on the cross for our sins and was resurrected on the third day🙏🏾. Also one must repent and turn away from thier sin. NOW, is the time to turn to JESUS, do it before its too LATE and the DOOR of Grace is Shut.

Dear Lord Jesus, I know that I am a sinner, and I ask for Your forgiveness. I believe You died for my sins and rose from the dead. I turn from my sins and invite You to come into my heart and life. I want to trust and follow You as my Lord and Savior.

textDisplay: We have a multitude of colored Knights in Arthurs court: Half-saracen Sir Feirefiz, nephew of Arthur and half-brother of Sir Parcival. Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers all were Knights of the Round Table and they all were Saracens. And then we have Sir Morien, who was a black half-moor. This makes 6 Arthurian Knights of Color. Even Arthur is often believed to be half-Roman and due to the influence of the Roman Invasion, different ethnical backgrounds and skin colors in this already fictional court are even more likely.

textDisplay: @seekeroftheway We have a multitude of colored Knights in Arthurs court: Half-saracen Sir Feirefiz, nephew of Arthur and half-brother of Sir Parcival. Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers all were Knights of the Round Table and they all were Saracens. And then we have Sir Morien, who was a black half-moor. This makes 6 Arthurian Knights of Color. Even Arthur is often believed to be half-Roman and due to the influence of the Roman Invasion, different ethnical backgrounds and skin colors in this already fictional court are even more likely.
textOriginal: @seekeroftheway We have a multitude of colored Knights in Arthurs court: Half-saracen Sir Feirefiz, nephew of Arthur and half-brother of Sir Parcival. textDisplay:
.For what i received i passed on to you as of first importance: that Christ died for our sins according to the Scriptures, that He was buried, that He was raised on the third day according to the scriptures  1 Corinthians 15 3 .


Lord Jesus fulfilled many many prophecy's- this is the meaning of "according to the Scriptures" . He also made many miracles like healings, exorcisms, resurrection, and control over nature.
Look at the resurrections.
 All four canonical gospels describe the resurrection of Jesus; three of them also relate a separate occasion on which Jesus calls a dead person back to life:


    Daughter of Jairus.‹See ›[Mk 5:21–43] Jairus, a major patron of a synagogue, asks Jesus to heal his daughter, but while Jesus is on the way, Jairus is told his daughter has died. Jesus tells him she was only sleeping, and wakes her with the words Talitha kum!
    The Young Man from Nain.‹See ›[Lk 7:11–17] A young man, the son of a widow, is brought out for burial in Nain. Jesus sees her, and his pity causes him to tell her not to cry. Jesus approaches the coffin and tells the man inside to get up, and he does so.
    The Raising of Lazarus.‹See ›[Jn 11:1–44] A close friend of Jesus who had been dead for four days is brought back to life when Jesus commands him to get up

    Daughter of Jairus.‹See ›[Mk 5:21–43] Jairus, a major patron of a synagogue, asks Jesus to heal his daughter, but while Jesus is on the way, Jairus is told his daughter has died. Jesus tells him she was only sleeping, and wakes her with the words Talitha kum!
    The Young Man from Nain.‹See ›[Lk 7:11–17] A young man, the son of a widow, is brought out for burial in Nain. Jesus sees her, and his pity causes him to tell her not to cry. Jesus approaches the coffin and tells the man inside to get up, and he does so.
    The Raising of Lazarus.‹See ›[Jn 11:1–44] A close friend of Jesus who had been dead for four days is brought back to life when Jesus commands him to get up

textDisplay: @midnightexpress8347 We have a multitude of colored Knights in Arthurs court: Half-saracen Sir Feirefiz, nephew of Arthur and half-brother of Sir Parcival. Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers all were Knights of the Round Table and they all were Saracens. And then we have Sir Morien, who was a black half-moor. This makes 6 Arthurian Knights of Color. Even Arthur is often believed to be half-Roman and due to the influence of the Roman Invasion, different ethnical backgrounds and skin colors in this already fictional court are even more likely.

textDisplay: Is the Green Knight meant to be a demon, a pagan god, a spirit or something else? Did the original story every elaborate on what he’s supposed to be?
textOriginal: Is the Green Knight meant to be a demon, a pagan god, a spirit or something else? Did the original story every elaborate on what he’s supposed to be?

textDisplay: There's no definitive answer in the text but there is a multitude of scholarly theories on it. We can go through them, if you happen to be more interested in it.
textOriginal: There's no definitive answer in the text but there is a multitude of scholarly theories on it. We can go through them, if you happen to be more interested in it.

textDisplay: @terriblereading7586 I’m interested in hearing what you have to say. You can be as detailed or concise as you want and I’d like to hear your take on it too.
textOriginal: @terriblereading7586 I’m interested in hearing what you have to say. You can be as detailed or concise as you want and I’d like to hear your take on it too.

textDisplay: ​@Garrus1995 Well, to quote Charles Long: "They have identified him as a Celtic fairy, as a former Celtic solar deity, as a shape-shifting demon, a demon from fairyland, a hunter, a devil or a fiend, as the Lord Prince of Hades, as an anti-Christ, or as Christ or God himself, as an angel, as Death, as an executioner, a Holly Knight, the Jack in the Green, or even as an historical squire, a count or a noble, or as a chthonic being, or as an untamed natural force, or Nature, or a vegetation spirit, or even as Gawain's shadow or subconscious, or the unlimited energy of symbol." And then he adds "It could also be Merlin". We can go trough each and everyone of those or we just look at the ones, that are particulary interesting to you.
textOriginal: ​@Garrus1995 Well, to quote Charles Long: "They have identified him as a Celtic fairy, as a former Celtic solar deity, as a shape-shifting demon, a demon from fairyland, a hunter, a devil or a fiend, as the Lord Prince of Hades, as an anti-Christ, or as Christ or God himself, as an angel, as Death, as an executioner, a Holly Knight, the Jack in the Green, or even as an historical squire, a count or a noble, or as a chthonic being, or as an untamed natural force, or Nature, or a vegetation spirit, or even as Gawain's shadow or subconscious, or the unlimited energy of symbol." And then he adds "It could also be Merlin". We can go trough each and everyone of those or we just look at the ones, that are particulary interesting to you.

textDisplay: @terriblereading7586 It seems like a lot of those things he could represent are contradictory. He could be a vegetation spirit but also could be an allegory for Christ? How are those anything alike?
textOriginal: @terriblereading7586 It seems like a lot of those things he could represent are contradictory. He could be a vegetation spirit but also could be an allegory for Christ? How are those anything alike?

textDisplay: @Garrus1995 Well, i am glad that you are asking. First of all, bear in mind that all of the above are just theories by different authors and scholars. Long just compiled them to show the multitude of those contradicting ideas. The vegetation spirit was actually one of the first scholarly interpretations of the Knight. E.K. Chambers for example identified him as "The green man of the peasantry, who dies and lives again, reappears as the Green Knight in one of the most famous divisions of Arthurian romance." in 1903,  in 1906 A.B. Cook thought the Knight was related to the ancient italian tree god Virbius and so on. These readings come from a either a folkloristic or a mythological background, whereas readings identifying the Green Knight as a Christ-like figure or an allegory for Christ obviously stem from readings that incorporate a stronger christian influence. Evidence, if us scholars of literature may call it that, for this reading is a bit more convoluted but it basically breaks down to "The Green Knight appears at Christmas, he overcomes death and he challenges the devout Gawain, wearing a Pentacle/Pentagram, to do as he did and be faithful that Death can be overcome". There's also the importance of the Green Chapel, the significance of the number 3 and other details, one could interpret as evidence for this idea. The problem is: the original poem is full of pagan elements and christian elements, just like every or most of the Arthurian stories. In "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight" Gawain even incorporates both of those ideas in his armor before the final encounter with the Green Knight, wearing his Pentacle AND the green Girdle given to him by Lady Hautdesert. To sum it up: Both ideas are alike in that they both stand for renewal, rebirth, spring and the act of overcoming death.

Chambers for example identified him as "The green man of the peasantry, who dies and lives again, reappears as the Green Knight in one of the most famous divisions of Arthurian romance." in 1903,  in 1906 A.B. Cook thought the Knight was related to the ancient italian tree god Virbius and so on. These readings come from a either a folkloristic or a mythological background, whereas readings identifying the Green Knight as a Christ-like figure or an allegory for Christ obviously stem from readings that incorporate a stronger christian influence. Evidence, if us scholars of literature may call it that, for this reading is a bit more convoluted but it basically breaks down to "The Green Knight appears at Christmas, he overcomes death and he challenges the devout Gawain, wearing a Pentacle/Pentagram, to do as he did and be faithful that Death can be overcome". There's also the importance of the Green Chapel, the significance of the number 3 and other details, one could interpret as evidence for this idea. The problem is: the original poem is full of pagan elements and christian elements, just like every or most of the Arthurian stories. In "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight" Gawain even incorporates both of those ideas in his armor before the final encounter with the Green Knight, wearing his Pentacle AND the green Girdle given to him by Lady Hautdesert.
