textDisplay: @terriblereading7586 That quote seems to go in a lot of different directions assigning everything to the Green Knight and one of them will stick.   I'd boil it it down to this - Morgan le Fay sends the Green Knight to test Arthur's resolve, she's a bitter half sister that likes to screw with Arthur, that's the basic surface plot.  The Green Knight represent regrowth he is green and this is medieval lit, sow the seed and harvest crop.  But it's not that simple, there's trials to get to the truth.  In the end the sash is worn by all the knights to remind them to be honest and be Knightly.  I don't think it's a very complicated story but with our times the next generations has to claim dominance over anything they can.  The old ways are trash.   I'm sure I'm completely wrong.    it will be an action packed movie and that's all anyone really cares about.  Glad you're reading

textDisplay:
It's strange to me too. They chose an Indian actor to play Sir Gawain, a historical figure arguably only along Gaelic/Celtic/Anglo-Saxon bloodlines. There's little room if any to interpret his appearance as that of an Indian person. It also doesn't help that he's the nephew of King Arthur and son of King Lot of Lothian/Morgause of Orkney, none of which are said to have blood ties to Indian ancestry.

Tbh, I wouldn't even really mind as long as Dev performs the role admirably and respectfully. It just boggles my mind how this would become an outrage if the script was flipped and it was a white actor taking the lead role in a historically/culturally Indian tale. Where's the consistency in casting for historical/cultural films?

textDisplay:
@Vampire285 
To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
@L0rd_Ju66y  They chose an Indian actor to play Sir Gawain, a historical figure arguably only along Gaelic/Celtic/Anglo-Saxon bloodlines. There's little room if any to interpret his appearance as that of a clearly Indian person. It also doesn't help that he's the nephew of King Arthur and son of King Lot of Lothian/Morgause of Orkney, none of which could reasonably have Indian ancestry/relations extrapolated from them.

I'm not saying it's impossible or bad to have a dark skinned character to be a lead in an Arthurian tale, in fact that'd be really dope. It's just that considering the historical texts given, choosing Sir Gawain as that character is a rather puzzling choice.
textOriginal:
@L0rd_Ju66y  They chose an Indian actor to play Sir Gawain, a historical figure arguably only along Gaelic/Celtic/Anglo-Saxon bloodlines. There's little room if any to interpret his appearance as that of a clearly Indian person. It also doesn't help that he's the nephew of King Arthur and son of King Lot of Lothian/Morgause of Orkney, none of which could reasonably have Indian ancestry/relations extrapolated from them.

I'm not saying it's impossible or bad to have a dark skinned character to be a lead in an Arthurian tale, in fact that'd be really dope. It's just that considering the historical texts given, choosing Sir Gawain as that character is a rather puzzling choice.

textDisplay:
@mehcaca We have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
Hmm... someone has noticed what this story is about. 
A story that is different according to how the reader's wits perform. 
The Green Knight should vary as to the seasons (and perhaps he does in this film), but, yes, this might actually be way out of the superheroes and back to the myths that bred them. 
Let's have The Mabinogion, Chuchulain and the Bulls, Jack of the Green, all the Celtic Myths from the arrival of the flying ships to Arthur, Robin Hood and beyond.

textDisplay: By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation. 🥱
textOriginal: By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation. 🥱

textDisplay:
We have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay: King Arthur and his Knights were Celtic (white), not black or brown.
textOriginal: King Arthur and his Knights were Celtic (white), not black or brown.

textDisplay:
At the time the Arthur myths were widely known in Britannia, the Romans had North African cavalry units here, they were a mix of Roman (Mediterranean) and North African (sub-saharan 'black'). 
-and the Romans reported in writings that have survived to the present that the people of Brittania, Caledonia and Hibernia especially the 'pictii' (painted or tattoed people) were a 'sallow' or yellow colour in their opinion. 
Arthur is from a later time than legends like Gawain and the Green Knight; both are based in Celtic Myth, yes, but present-day standards and definitions cannot apply backwards over thousands of years.

textDisplay: By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation. 🥱
textOriginal: By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation. 🥱

textDisplay:
We have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay: For people complaining about the woke casting of Dev Patel, as much as i personally dont care about who is playing who - Dev Patel is fantastic and it is a fantasy epic -, i should point out that even in the film's own canon the casting is impossible. Gawain is Arthur's nephew, Arthur and everyone else are shown as white. it is therefore impossible that Gawain is of Indian ethnicity. does it effect the film at all for me? no. but if the film was truly woke, Arthur would be Indian.

textDisplay:
Gawain and the Green Knight is far older than Arthur. 
The legend of Arthur contains Gawain's story and the turning of the seasons as metaphor for a lifetime, but it came later and absorbed this older myth. 
Another example is the magic cauldron 'that none left unsatisfied'; much older than Arthur, but he has it at Camelot.
textOriginal:
Gawain and the Green Knight is far older than Arthur. 
The legend of Arthur contains Gawain's story and the turning of the seasons as metaphor for a lifetime, but it came later and absorbed this older myth. 
Another example is the magic cauldron 'that none left unsatisfied'; much older than Arthur, but he has it at Camelot.

textDisplay:
To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
@datrute To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
@randommoments3806 To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
Brownwashing is not acceptable. Stories inspired by British legend and folklore should be portrayed by people who can accurately represent a white ethnic group. 

Our heritage is so important to us, yet we are being written out of it in an effort to strip is of a sense of ownership and belonging. 

Still, we’re left with all the “bad” things our ancestors did: POC don’t seem to want “diverse” portrayals of unsavoury history or culture, they only want to take the things that have inspired our people over centuries.

textDisplay:
Please read this carefully ;

Every one of us has sinned against God. We’ve lied, we’ve stolen, we’ve sinned sexually, and we’ve taken His name in vain; which makes us liars, thieves, fornicators and blasphemers ... Yet this is only four of our many transgressions .

The problem is, we can never ‘outweigh’ our sin with good works because God is perfect in Holiness, meaning his standard is beyond our reach. He is also perfect in Justice, meaning, he will bring EVERY sin to light. The punishment for our sin is death, and Hell; a place of Eternal Fire, where there will be weeping and gnashing of teeth .

Fortunately God is also perfect in Love, so 2000 years ago He left his throne in heaven, and came to earth as a man named Jesus Christ. While here, he lived a perfectly righteous life, only to take the punishment that We deserved; Our sinless creator became Our sin, and endured a gruesome death on a cross for our sake . 
Right before he died, he said ‘it is finished’ .. meaning, the debt for our sins has been paid in full. And on the third day, he defeated death and rose from the grave. So now, we can go free from the wrath of God’s judgement not by earning it, but rather, as a free gift of grace ..

Except gifts can be either accepted or rejected. So this is what you must do in response ; 

Humbly admit to God that you have sinned against him. Then, confess with your mouth that Jesus Christ is Lord, Your savior, and the ruler of your life from this moment forward ( Romans 10:9 ) . 

Do these things, and you will be saved .

If you do so sincerely, God will grant you a new heart which no longer finds comfort in sin, but instead desires to read and obey his word. He will also grant you Eternal life in his presence; The greatest pleasure on earth doesn’t even come close to what God is preparing for believers in the afterlife  . 

You’ve been informed ; Now the choice is yours. What you do with this information will determine where you will be for ETERNITY.. Meaning, this is the single most critical decision you will EVER make. 

Jesus Christ awaits you this very moment. Do not put off surrendering your life to him, because your tomorrow isn’t guaranteed... and as it is written ( Hebrews 9 : 27 )  ;

‘It is appointed unto man ONCE to die, but after this, THE JUDGMENT’ .

textDisplay: To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain: By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain.  The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation .  And I hear you say " Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century . " Well , there is also a half - black Arthurian Knight in the most important Medieval Germanic Arthurian Myth .  His name is Feirefiz and he is the half - brother of Sir Parcival.  Also Patel is a British man and citizen , portraying a medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys.  And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men.  And male bisexuality .  I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay: Sure it does.   Check out Sir Palamedes, or his brothers Sir Safir and Segwarides, for example.  Or Sir Priamus.  Arthur's knights came from from all over, including Africa and the Middle East.   Nothing far-fetched about a knight with Indian descent, (especially since he's also born and raised in England).  Many people traveled widely, 1500 years ago, especially knights/soldiers.  The Arthur legends reflected that, and mostly just the more recent  (19th/20th century) retellings that tended to forget it.
textOriginal: Sure it does.   Check out Sir Palamedes, or his brothers Sir Safir and Segwarides, for example.  Or Sir Priamus.  Arthur's knights came from from all over, including Africa and the Middle East.   Nothing far-fetched about a knight with Indian descent, (especially since he's also born and raised in England).  Many people traveled widely, 1500 years ago, especially knights/soldiers.  The Arthur legends reflected that, and mostly just the more recent  (19th/20th century) retellings that tended to forget it.

textDisplay: @BlueCanary7 Sir Palamedes makes sense. Because of the crusades, plenty of contacts happened between Europe and northern Middle East. And Romans had contact with Africa even before the Middle Ages. But India? No. The first European to set foot in India came hundreds of years later... even if by miracle a Indian found his way into Europe in the Middle Ages, the chances he somehow integrates into society and becoming a knight is around -1.
textOriginal: @BlueCanary7 Sir Palamedes makes sense. Because of the crusades, plenty of contacts happened between Europe and northern Middle East. And Romans had contact with Africa even before the Middle Ages. But India? No. The first European to set foot in India came hundreds of years later... even if by miracle a Indian found his way into Europe in the Middle Ages, the chances he somehow integrates into society and becoming a knight is around -1.

textDisplay:
@ka-boom2083 Numerous Greeks and Romans were traveling to/trading with India centuries before Arthur, with a well-established spice trade.  It's really not that far-fetched, given Britain was also part of the Roman Empire.  And we certainly have records of notable, early Indian travelers crossing continents, (eg Bodhisena, who went East rather than West, and in the 1400s, Guru Nanak Sahib, who traveled much of Asia but also reached Rome).  
But more to the point,  while I've no doubt the crusades inspired  Palamedes et al., King Arthur's Court is supposed to be set in Sub-Roman Britain, 500 years before the first crusade.  The crusades are no more historically correct for these stories than plate armour, stone castles, jousting, etc.   (If you want to be truly historically accurate, you've also got to drop the round table, most of the knights - including Gawain - and a king called "Arthur".)    
But from a literary point of view, including some "knights from far away places" served a purpose, making the glory of King Arthur's court bigger than Britain, with tales of their valor drawing in the greatest even from distant lands.  In creating these knights, writers chose the "far-away places" relevant to their own era, (not Arthur's).  And in that way, casting Dev Patel is absolutely in keeping with the original stories. 
(Though frankly I think he's a great casting choice regardless.  In the trailer, he looks like he's inhabiting the role perfectly.  Can't wait to see it!)

It's really not that far-fetched, given Britain was also part of the Roman Empire.  And we certainly have records of notable, early Indian travelers crossing continents, (eg Bodhisena, who went East rather than West, and in the 1400s, Guru Nanak Sahib, who traveled much of Asia but also reached Rome).  
But more to the point,  while I've no doubt the crusades inspired  Palamedes et al., King Arthur's Court is supposed to be set in Sub-Roman Britain, 500 years before the first crusade.  The crusades are no more historically correct for these stories than plate armour, stone castles, jousting, etc.   (If you want to be truly historically accurate, you've also got to drop the round table, most of the knights - including Gawain - and a king called "Arthur".)    
But from a literary point of view, including some "knights from far away places" served a purpose, making the glory of King Arthur's court bigger than Britain, with tales of their valor drawing in the greatest even from distant lands.  In creating these knights, writers chose the "far-away places" relevant to their own era, (not Arthur's).  And in that way, casting Dev Patel is absolutely in keeping with the original stories. 
(Though frankly I think he's a great casting choice regardless.  In the trailer, he looks like he's inhabiting the role perfectly.  Can't wait to see it!)

textDisplay:
To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay: Dev Patel's casting is totally in line with the original stories.   Check out Sir Palamedes, or his brothers Safir and Segwarides, for example.  Or Sir Priamus.  The knights came from from all over, including Africa and the Middle East.   Nothing far-fetched about a knight with Indian descent, (especially since he's also born and raised in England).
textOriginal: Dev Patel's casting is totally in line with the original stories.   Check out Sir Palamedes, or his brothers Safir and Segwarides, for example.  Or Sir Priamus.  The knights came from from all over, including Africa and the Middle East.   Nothing far-fetched about a knight with Indian descent, (especially since he's also born and raised in England).

textDisplay: @BlueCanary7 Holy shit, you're actually going to twist the mythology to justify modern globalist fuckery? Esclabor, Palamedes, Safir, and Segwarides were exiled Saracen nobility. Meaning Arab or Turkic Muslim. Ser Gawain was from the northern fringes of the British isles. He was not Arabian or from North Africa. There were no Indians on the round table. Furthermore, Dev Patel has no more business inserting himself into our mythology than a Caucasian like myself has any business inserting myself into his ancestral mythology
textOriginal: @BlueCanary7 Holy shit, you're actually going to twist the mythology to justify modern globalist fuckery? Esclabor, Palamedes, Safir, and Segwarides were exiled Saracen nobility. Meaning Arab or Turkic Muslim. Ser Gawain was from the northern fringes of the British isles. He was not Arabian or from North Africa. There were no Indians on the round table. Furthermore, Dev Patel has no more business inserting himself into our mythology than a Caucasian like myself has any business inserting myself into his ancestral mythology

textDisplay: @rfarrr2817 "Twisting mythology"?   No, I'm saying that having a  few knights from far away places was always part of the legends,  showing Arthur's greatness was known 'across the world' and drew the very best knights to him, including Saracens, Phrigians, etc.   Casting Dev Patel as one of Arthur's knights makes sense as a continuation of that tradition.  Not a globalist claim.   Just a  literary one.
textOriginal: @rfarrr2817 "Twisting mythology"?   No, I'm saying that having a  few knights from far away places was always part of the legends,  showing Arthur's greatness was known 'across the world' and drew the very best knights to him, including Saracens, Phrigians, etc.   Casting Dev Patel as one of Arthur's knights makes sense as a continuation of that tradition.  Not a globalist claim.   Just a  literary one.

textDisplay:
@freedomisslavery6840 To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.
textOriginal:
@freedomisslavery6840 To all the people complaining about Patel as Gawain:

It's based on a 14th century poem.
By the 12th century we have court records of Saracen warriors settled in southwest England, and certainly in the 8th century there is numismatic evidence of contact between England and Moorish Spain. The filmmakers have made a reasonable extrapolation.
And I hear you say "Boohoo but Arthurs fictional stories take place between the 4th and 6th century." 
Even better: the Roman Invasion at that time introduced a wide variety of ethnicities and skin complexions to the British Isles through the Roman Army. In fact Arthur might have been half-roman (if and that's a big IF he ever even existed and because Gawain is Arthurs nephew, well figure that one out for yourself), as far as scholars are concerned  and we have a multitude of dark-skinned Knights in the court of Arthur in the Medieval Arthurian Stories: we have the 4 full Saracens - Sir Palomedes, his father and his two brothers, we have Sir Feirefiz who is half-Saracen and Sir Parcivals half-brother as well as Arthurs nephew. And we have Sir Morien, who is black and half-moorish. These are all established knights of the Round Table and some of their stories are a hundred years older than "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight". Arthur's court has never ever been only white. Check the stories.
And you will cry "But Gawain is none of the above! And Saracens are not Indians!"
Well, great that Patel is neither playing a Saracen nor an Indian knight.
He is a British man and citizen, portraying an medieval knight with a skin complexion only slightly darker than the average Caucasian - a weathered look a knight could acquire from long journeys. 
And in case you forgot: it's based on a fictional 14th century fantasy romance poem full of dragons, witches and green men. And male bisexuality. 
I'd say quit whining.

textDisplay:
It's based on "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight", a story involving the legendary King Arthur and his knights. A mysterious green knight approaches Arthur's court and issues a challenge. Along the lines of "Let one of your knights try to land a blow on me. I will return in a year to land a blow on them." 

Some folks think it's a trick, but Sir Gawain accepts. The green knight doesn't even try to avoid the blow so Gawain ends up cutting off his head. But then the knight simply picks up his head, promises to return in a year, and rides away. Anything more than that would probably spoil the movie.

As far as why the Green Knight looks like a tree or can survive having his head cut off, he's probably based on older stories about the Green Man. The Green Man is a legendary nature spirit associated with rebirth (think plants after winter) who looks like a man made of wood and leaves.
textOriginal:
It's based on "Sir Gawain and the Green Knight", a story involving the legendary King Arthur and his knights. A mysterious green knight approaches Arthur's court and issues a challenge. Some folks think it's a trick, but Sir Gawain accepts. The green knight doesn't even try to avoid the blow so Gawain ends up cutting off his head. But then the knight simply picks up his head, promises to return in a year, and rides away. Anything more than that would probably spoil the movie.

As far as why the Green Knight looks like a tree or can survive having his head cut off, he's probably based on older stories about the Green Man. The Green Man is a legendary nature spirit associated with rebirth (think plants after winter) who looks like a man made of wood and leaves.

textDisplay:
Sir Gawain was white, not Indian. Arthurian legends are about white European people. Always has been. Even within just the world of this particular movie it's distracting that the main hero is non-white whilst every other character around him looks to be white. It's clear this is another example of them erasing parts of foundational white European culture. They're erasing the identity of Sir Gawain, to be honest. According to the woke leftist cult heroes can't be white males anymore, because white males are the #1 enemy. And bonus virtue signal points are awarded to those who erase the identity of legendary white heroes. They delight in trolling anyone who doesn't feel guilty for being white.

Couldn't they come up with new original stories with new non-white heroes, if they desire them so bad? Are they afraid no one would watch it? They piggy-back on enduring European legends while simultaneously insulting and shitting all over those legends. It's vile. Why is every culture but white culture safeguarded?  It's a shame because it looks interesting. Imagine if Marvel recast the Black Panther with a white male. Don't deny there would be outrage over it. This is similarly a dumb incomprehensible decision, but considerably more harmful considering the lasting impact and importance of Arthurian legends. Unwatchable garbage. How about just leaving white people and their culture alone?

textDisplay: It looks like there are going to be some creative liberties taken for the second half of the story, as I don't recall there being giants and a talking fox, but these added elements could only serve to help the narrative. If I recall the second half of the book is kind of lack luster; Sir Gawain goes to find the green knight. finds a kingdom that knows where that cathedral is. the king of the land begs him to stay for a weekend's feast. the kings wife tried to seduce gawain for over the weekend while the king is out hunting wild game. then when gawain reaches the cathedral, the green knights says, "gg I was the king all along. thx for not porking my wife. you passed the test." Then Sir Gawain is crowned king. and everyone lived happily ever after.
textOriginal: It looks like there are going to be some creative liberties taken for the second half of the story, as I don't recall there being giants and a talking fox, but these added elements could only serve to help the narrative. textDisplay: @Rodark_GR The poem briefly mentions Gawains encounters with all sorts of fantastic beings and fiends, so I'd say GoT in that sense, although I must confess that I haven't read/watched GoT yet. We might see some knight fights tho, as chivalry and being a honorable knight is an important motif of the poem. If you want, I can check for a nice modern day English translation (since the original is from the 14th century) available for free online.
textOriginal: @Rodark_GR The poem briefly mentions Gawains encounters with all sorts of fantastic beings and fiends, so I'd say GoT in that sense, although I must confess that I haven't read/watched GoT yet. textDisplay:
@Rodark_GR I wrote an essay about for my uni course.

Just to hype you up a little more:
J.R.R. Tolkien was an important scholar in the field of English Medieval Literature and he and a colleague translated the poem aswell and they examined it. Tolkiens own works are also inspired by it and other texts like Beowulf. 

So, it's like meeting the old mysterious (grand)father of LotR and The Hobbit.
textOriginal:
@Rodark_GR I wrote an essay about for my uni course.

Just to hype you up a little more:
J.R.R. Tolkien was an important scholar in the field of English Medieval Literature and he and a colleague translated the poem aswell and they examined it. Tolkiens own works are also inspired by it and other texts like Beowulf. 

So, it's like meeting the old mysterious (grand)father of LotR and The Hobbit.

textDisplay: This is literally based on a medieval  fantasy poem in which it is perfectly understandable for a knight to have green skin, hair and a green horse. It is a world of fantasy in which a belt on your waist saves you from being beheaded, a world of dragons, ogres and witches. Take your racism elsewhere. Neither the poem nor the movie are retellings of real life events.
textOriginal: This is literally based on a medieval  fantasy poem in which it is perfectly understandable for a knight to have green skin, hair and a green horse. It is a world of fantasy in which a belt on your waist saves you from being beheaded, a world of dragons, ogres and witches. Take your racism elsewhere. Neither the poem nor the movie are retellings of real life events.

textDisplay: @terriblereading7586 The Green Knight is based on Arthurian mythology. The mythology isn't "real" but it has an intrinsic root with English identity, based on a time where there were no people of Indian ethnicity in England. It's not racist to be historically accurate, even when it comes to legendary material. It's like a white European playing Vishnu.
textOriginal: @terriblereading7586 The Green Knight is based on Arthurian mythology. The mythology isn't "real" but it has an intrinsic root with English identity, based on a time where there were no people of Indian ethnicity in England. It's not racist to be historically accurate, even when it comes to legendary material. It's like a white European playing Vishnu.

textDisplay: @MyHerpderp I'm not saying it's racist to be historically accurate but it is not a problem to have Patel play Gawain: he's not playing an Indian character in the movie's universe.
textOriginal: @MyHerpderp I'm not saying it's racist to be historically accurate but it is not a problem to have Patel play Gawain: he's not playing an Indian character in the movie's universe.
